tweets.text:
$MU $EWY C-HBM4E Base Logic Die Showdown

Samsung is going it alone, betting its 4nm in-house logic and 1C DRAM is good enough to compete while keeping costs down. I think that's the riskier bet. TSMC has spent years building high-performance logic dies for the world's most demanding AI GPUs. Samsung hasn't. And with custom HBM4E integrating memory controllers directly into the stack, the performance bar is higher than ever.

IMO, SK Hynix and Micron made the safer and smarter call. TSMC's 3nm logic is worth the cost and complexity, especially when winning Nvidia is the prize.

Maybe Samsung has a trick up its sleeve. Maybe this is a quiet attempt to revive its struggling foundry business. Either way, we'll see.

profile_bio.description: Data Scientist & Memory Engineer. Masters Degree @UofIllinois 2x🎓 Founder of Smart Candle System, FREE trial on Patreon 👇

quoted_tweet.text:
$MU Custom HBM4E (C-HBM4E). Literally, the most important HBM transformation that nobody is talking about.

HBM4E is already the next leap beyond HBM4. Faster. Denser. Built on a TSMC base die that acts as the foundation connecting all the DRAM stacks above it.

C-HBM4E takes it further.

Historically the memory controller lived on the GPU. Custom HBM4E moves it directly into the base die. Shorter signal paths. Tighter integration. The GPU and memory are essentially one unit now.

2.5x performance uplift vs previous generations.

2x better power efficiency.

More complex to build. More expensive. But for hyperscalers running AI at scale, performance per watt is everything.

This is not an incremental update. It's a architectural shift in how memory and compute work together.

The collaborations between GPU and Memory companies will grow in the over the next decade and one day, we might actually have a GPU-in-Memory, a total and seamless integration.

profile_bio.description: Data Scientist & Memory Engineer. Masters Degree @UofIllinois 2x🎓 Founder of Smart Candle System, FREE trial on Patreon 👇
